steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question testing matching punctuation around an interrupting phrase. The comma already placed after “become” is the decisive clue: a comma that opens a parenthetical interruption must be closed by a matching comma.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "Gothic—": An em dash cannot pair with an opening comma. Mismatched delimiters leave the interrupting phrase improperly enclosed. ✗
  • "Gothic;": A semicolon connects two independent clauses; it cannot close a mid-sentence interrupting phrase, and the clause that follows (“best known as a novelist”) is not independent. ✗
  • "Gothic": No punctuation at all leaves the phrase “with the breakout success of her 2020 novel Mexican Gothic” unclosed, fusing it awkwardly into the main clause. ✗
  • "Gothic,": A comma matches the opening comma after “become,” correctly enclosing the interrupting prepositional phrase on both sides. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
